target: WIN-061 detail_evidence in data/wins.json — two targeted substring patches resolving the SPE-vs-geomagnetic-storm category conflation flagged in WIN-061.c4 hole H5 (ISS-1451). PATCH A reorders paragraph 1 so Pazos et al. 2019 (genuine CME-driven G-class storm observations at Coeneo) leads as the primary storm-time enhancement evidence, with Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 (solar proton events at Arrival Heights) presented as a distinct-but-related disturbance class showing the same direction; an explicit mechanistic note distinguishes SPE-class proton precipitation from CME-storm electron precipitation. PATCH B rewords the paragraph-2 S&F reference to stop implying that S&F itself is storm-time evidence, adds a cross-reference to Pazos as the genuine CME-storm citation, and avoids the 'cavity-Q enhancement' phrase being read as a storm-time claim. The ~22 Hz baseline argument, the V51.1 falsification-acknowledgement kernel-of-truth credit, the Section 2.1 cross-reference, the Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 mechanism-menu citation, the three-dimension (direction/magnitude/timing) framing, and the Unscored-vs-observed internal-contradiction closing paragraph are all preserved verbatim. TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are not touched (already directionally consistent with the rewrite).
source_issue: ISS-1451 (moderate H5) — SPE vs geomagnetic-storm category conflation in paragraph 1 and paragraph 2 of WIN-061 detail_evidence
patch_instructions: Two edits to WIN-061 detail_evidence in data/wins.json. Apply PATCH A (paragraph 1, two-sentence reorder + mechanistic parenthetical) first, then PATCH B (paragraph 2, one-sentence S&F reference rewording). Each before-string is verified unique in the current detail_evidence field as of the run timestamp. After integration, verify: (a) all three DOI links still resolve — 10.1029/1999JA900056 (S&F 1999, appears twice, same text in both locations), 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 (Pazos 2019, now appears twice after integration), 10.1007/978-3-662-03137-4_11 (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002, unchanged); (b) the '#part2' onclick='showTab(selftest)' Section 2.1 cross-reference remains intact (not touched by either patch); (c) the H_eff ≈ 3,400 km baseline sentence and the ~22 Hz arithmetic are preserved verbatim; (d) the V51.1 Update paragraph heading 'V51.1 Update:' is preserved verbatim. Character delta: PATCH A +455, PATCH B +159, total +614. Projected new detail_evidence length: 5138 (current: 4524). TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are NOT modified by this EXP — they currently frame the argument around the ~22 Hz baseline overshoot and a direction-mixed literature characterization, which this patch reinforces rather than contradicts.

patch_anchors.before: Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 (JGR 104:10111–10118) measured damping decrease (Q increase) and frequency increase during solar proton events at Arrival Heights — i.e., cavity-Q enhancement, not suppression. Pazos et al. 2019 (JASTP 193:105091) reported statistically significant amplitude increases at Coeneo during moderate-to-severe storms.
patch_anchors.after: The most direct storm-time evidence is Pazos et al. 2019 (JASTP 193:105091), which reports statistically significant first-mode amplitude increases at Coeneo during moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms (Dst ≲ −70 nT) — enhancement, not suppression. A distinct but related disturbance class, solar proton events (SPEs), shows the same direction: Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 (JGR 104:10111–10118) measured damping decrease (Q increase) and frequency increase during SPEs at Arrival Heights — cavity-Q enhancement. (SPE and CME-driven geomagnetic storms differ mechanistically — proton precipitation deepens to mesospheric altitudes while CME-driven electron precipitation couples more strongly to the D-region — so SPE direction is supporting rather than primary evidence for G3 storm behaviour.)
patch_anchors.rationale: ISS-1451 (c4 H5, moderate): paragraph 1 previously led with S&F 1999 (an SPE study at Arrival Heights) immediately after the 'G3+ geomagnetic storms' framing sentence, collapsing the SPE/storm-class distinction. A sophisticated dome defender can argue that SPE evidence (proton precipitation deepening to mesospheric altitudes) is not logically applicable to G3 CME-driven storms (dominated by electron precipitation into the D-region) and that the text itself labels S&F as SPE-specific, so S&F should not be the primary citation rebutting storm-time suppression. The rewrite addresses this in three moves: (1) Pazos et al. 2019 — which studied genuine moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms at Coeneo (Dst ≲ −70 nT) — is promoted to the primary storm-time enhancement citation with the Dst criterion made explicit; (2) S&F 1999 is explicitly qualified as 'a distinct but related disturbance class' and its direction is presented as supporting rather than primary evidence; (3) a parenthetical sentence makes the SPE/storm mechanistic distinction explicit (proton vs electron precipitation, mesospheric vs D-region coupling), pre-empting the defender's attack rather than waiting for it. The three-citation structure (Pazos, S&F, Nickolaenko & Hayakawa) is preserved; only ordering and framing change. All other paragraph-1 content (opening well-documented sentence, mechanism sentence, Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 mechanism-menu reference, ~22 Hz baseline derivation, dome-has-no-equation conclusion, three-dimension framing, Section 2.1 cross-reference) is preserved verbatim.

patch_anchors.before: the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 paper often cited in the storm-time SR literature actually reports cavity-Q enhancement (damping decrease, frequency increase) during SPEs, not suppression.
patch_anchors.after: the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 paper often cited in storm-time SR discussions for its cavity-Q observations during high-energy particle precipitation actually reports enhancement (damping decrease, frequency increase) during SPEs — and the genuine-CME-storm evidence (Pazos et al. 2019, paragraph 1) likewise reports enhancement, not suppression.
patch_anchors.rationale: ISS-1451 (c4 H5 paragraph-2 component, moderate): paragraph 2's S&F reference previously read 'the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 paper often cited in the storm-time SR literature actually reports cavity-Q enhancement ... during SPEs, not suppression' — which still invites the SPE/storm conflation attack because the text describes S&F as a storm-time SR citation that then reports on SPEs. A dome defender could argue: if S&F is an SPE study, why is it cited as storm-time evidence? The rewrite resolves this by (1) changing 'often cited in the storm-time SR literature' to 'often cited in storm-time SR discussions for its cavity-Q observations during high-energy particle precipitation' — making explicit that the citation is for a specific observational finding rather than treating the paper as a storm-time study; (2) dropping 'cavity-Q enhancement' (which could be read as a storm-time claim) and using 'enhancement' alone; (3) appending a cross-reference to Pazos et al. 2019 as 'the genuine-CME-storm evidence' (paragraph 1) — closing the loop that the enhancement direction in the literature is supported by both SPE (S&F) and genuine storm (Pazos) evidence. The 'not suppression' conclusion is preserved. The sentence remains the second paragraph's refutation of the 'standard ionospheric physics predicts suppression' claim, but no longer rests the refutation on miscategorizing S&F as a storm-time study.

holes_addressed.description: Paragraph 1's top line is about 'G3+ geomagnetic storms', but the current paragraph leads with Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 — a solar proton event study at Arrival Heights — as the primary enhancement citation. Pazos et al. 2019 (genuine storms at Coeneo, Dst ≲ −70 nT) appears second and is undersold. A sophisticated dome defender can argue 'S&F 1999 is SPE, not CME storm — your own paragraph labels it SPE — why are you using SPE-enhancement evidence to rebut G3-storm suppression?' The curmudgeon c4 review flagged this as a moderate severity hole because the underlying evidence is correct (both studies report enhancement and the literature is mixed-direction) but the ordering and framing invite an avoidable category-error objection.
holes_addressed.fix: Pazos et al. 2019 promoted to lead citation with explicit Dst ≲ −70 nT (G-class storm criterion) and 'genuine CME-driven storm' framing implicit in the 'moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms' phrasing. S&F 1999 demoted to a supporting 'distinct but related disturbance class' citation with explicit 'SPE' label and explicit statement that SPE direction is 'supporting rather than primary evidence for G3 storm behaviour'. A parenthetical sentence makes the SPE/storm mechanistic distinction explicit (proton precipitation mesosphere vs electron precipitation D-region), pre-empting the defender objection rather than relying on the reader to infer it.

holes_addressed.description: Paragraph 2's S&F reference previously described the paper as 'often cited in the storm-time SR literature' — which, when combined with its 'actually reports cavity-Q enhancement ... during SPEs' clause, reads as claiming S&F is a storm-time study that happens to measure SPEs. A defender can argue that 'storm-time SR literature' and 'SPEs' are being equated rhetorically. The curmudgeon c4 review flagged this as a paragraph-2 component of the same H5 issue.
holes_addressed.fix: Replaced 'often cited in the storm-time SR literature' with 'often cited in storm-time SR discussions for its cavity-Q observations during high-energy particle precipitation' — making explicit that the citation is for the specific observational finding (cavity-Q behaviour during high-energy particle precipitation) rather than treating the paper as a storm-time study. Added an explicit cross-reference to Pazos et al. 2019 (paragraph 1) as the genuine CME-storm evidence, so the paragraph-2 refutation of 'standard ionospheric physics predicts suppression' now rests on both Pazos (genuine G-class storm, enhancement) and S&F (SPE, enhancement) in their correct categories. The 'not suppression' conclusion is preserved.

new_evidence_added: Dst ≲ −70 nT criterion made explicit for Pazos et al. 2019 — previously the text said 'moderate-to-severe storms' without the quantitative classification. Dst < ~−70 nT is the standard moderate-to-severe geomagnetic-storm threshold and matches the Pazos study's reported storm-selection criterion (referenced in data/predictions.json PRED-SR-SUPPRESS detail_reasoning).

Explicit mechanistic distinction between SPE and CME-driven geomagnetic storms — proton precipitation deepens to mesospheric altitudes; CME-driven electron precipitation couples more strongly to the D-region. This is not a new scientific claim; it is a standard textbook distinction (Schlegel & Füllekrug themselves discuss it, as do Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 chapter 6). Adding it to WIN-061 pre-empts a defender category-error attack.

Explicit 'supporting rather than primary evidence' framing for S&F — acknowledges the SPE/storm distinction without abandoning the S&F citation. The overall mixed-direction literature argument is preserved and strengthened because it no longer collapses a distinction the paragraph itself labels.

anticipated_objections.objection: Leading with a single-station, single-paper citation (Pazos et al. 2019, Coeneo Mexico) is itself fragile — one paper from one station is not a consensus finding, so promoting it to primary storm-time evidence overstates its weight.
anticipated_objections.response: Fair caveat. The rewrite does not claim Pazos establishes consensus — it says Pazos 'reports' enhancement at Coeneo, and the paragraph still invokes Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 as the mechanism-menu authority and explicitly states 'Suppression is reported elsewhere depending on storm phase, station latitude, and mode'. The purpose is not to elevate Pazos to definitive; it is to have the primary storm-time citation actually be a storm-time study. For a sharper framing, the text includes 'most direct storm-time evidence' (not 'definitive' or 'only') — language calibrated to its single-station scope.

anticipated_objections.objection: The SPE/CME mechanistic parenthetical (proton deepens to mesosphere, electron couples to D-region) is a textbook oversimplification. Real storm-time ionospheric response involves both populations in varying ratios, and the mesosphere/D-region split is a heuristic, not a sharp cutoff.
anticipated_objections.response: Accurate caveat. The rewrite uses 'deepens to' and 'couples more strongly to' (not 'only reaches' or 'exclusively') — language that preserves the heuristic framing. The Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 'mechanism menu' citation already points readers to the full mechanistic inventory. If the decider prefers a softer qualifier, 'tends to deepen' / 'tends to couple more strongly' are drop-in substitutes. The point is not mechanism-level precision; it is establishing that SPE and CME-storm conditions are mechanistically distinguishable and therefore SPE evidence is a supporting-rather-than-primary line for G3-storm claims — the qualitative point does the work.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding a parenthetical in paragraph 1 adds ~200 characters of reader cost for a moderate-severity polish issue. The detail_evidence is already 4524 chars; 5138 is approaching long-form essay length for a single WIN.
anticipated_objections.response: Legitimate length concern. The added material is the mechanistic distinction parenthetical (strengthens paragraph 1) plus the qualifier framing for S&F (prevents ISS-1451 from persisting). Alternative: a shorter version could drop the parenthetical and rely on 'distinct but related disturbance class' plus 'supporting rather than primary' to carry the distinction — that saves ~200 chars but weakens the pre-emption. Opinion: the length cost is justified because the parenthetical is the specific load-bearing phrase that makes the paragraph immune to the defender's category-error attack. If the decider disagrees, a shorter form is available: drop the parenthetical and rely on the qualifier phrases alone.

anticipated_objections.objection: 'Genuine CME-driven storm' in PATCH B and 'moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms' in PATCH A are redundant — the reader already knows from paragraph 1 that Pazos is storm-time; paragraph 2's cross-reference is belt-and-braces.
anticipated_objections.response: Agreed that the cross-reference is redundant in paragraph-isolation terms. It is included because collapsed-panel readers may skip paragraph 1 or read paragraphs in different orders; the cross-reference makes paragraph 2 self-contained. Cost: +~30 chars. Benefit: paragraph 2's S&F refutation no longer depends on the reader having absorbed paragraph 1. On the user-preference-applied other side: if the decider prefers tighter prose, striking 'and the genuine-CME-storm evidence (Pazos et al. 2019, paragraph 1)' from PATCH B would save ~65 chars without damaging the core S&F-deconflation fix.

anticipated_objections.objection: A fair-minded dome defender would note the rewrite still claims 'the direction of that response — enhancement vs suppression — is not uniform across events' from the preserved paragraph-1 opening, but then lists two enhancement papers (Pazos, S&F) and one mechanism-menu reference (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa) that is cited for its mechanism inventory. Where are the suppression-direction papers? Citing mixed-direction language while listing only enhancement-direction studies is one-sided stacking.
anticipated_objections.response: Real concern, partially addressed. The paragraph preserves the phrase 'Suppression is reported elsewhere depending on storm phase, station latitude, and mode — see Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 for the mechanism menu' — which does cite suppression-direction evidence (the Nickolaenko & Hayakawa chapter inventories multiple observational outcomes including suppression). The current ISS-1451 patch does not expand the suppression-direction citations because (a) the scope is SPE/storm conflation, not literature balance, and (b) the mixed-direction framing is factually correct — the literature is genuinely mixed, and the dome has picked a specific direction with no a-priori constraint. If a future curmudgeon cycle flags stacking more specifically, a follow-up EXP could add a specific suppression-direction citation (e.g., Sátori et al. 2005 or Price 2000). Not in scope for this patch.

kernel_of_truth: The c4 curmudgeon review correctly credits EXP-227 for a clean, narrow, well-scoped fix of the two most-exploitable c3 holes (S&F citation direction, backwards D-region mechanism). ISS-1451 is a side effect of that otherwise-correct rewrite: by correctly adding S&F's actual measurement direction (cavity-Q enhancement during SPEs), the rewrite introduced a category distinction (SPE vs CME-storm) that the paragraph labels but does not operationalize. A defender reading paragraph 1 as it stands can point out 'your own text calls S&F an SPE study but presents it as primary evidence against storm-time suppression' — a valid category-error objection even though the underlying mixed-direction argument is correct. This EXP does not retract any of EXP-227's substantive corrections; it adds the operational step the previous rewrite did not complete. The kernel-of-truth in the dome's V51.1 falsification acknowledgement is preserved unchanged.
why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Fixing the SPE/storm category conflation does not weaken the WIN-061 Self-Contradicted verdict: the ~22 Hz baseline overshoot (unchanged, independently supported by WIN-038) remains the primary verdict-carrying argument. The storm-time direction argument, after this patch, is (a) Pazos 2019 reports enhancement during genuine G-class storms, (b) S&F 1999 reports enhancement during SPEs (same direction, related mechanism), (c) Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 documents the full mechanism menu including suppression under other conditions — all correctly categorized. The dome's PRED-SR-SUPPRESS remains a direction-and-magnitude assertion with no a-priori constraint and the ~22 Hz baseline critique remains independent of any storm-time story.
cross_references: monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-061.c4.json — c4 holes_found H5 (this EXP's target), plus H1/H2/H3/H4/H6/H7/H8 (out of scope for this EXP; some already decider-applied, others pending separate EXPs)

monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-227-WIN061-SF-citation-direction.json — upstream EXP that introduced the current paragraph-1 structure; this EXP builds on EXP-227's citation-direction fix rather than replacing it

data/wins.json WIN-038 (Schumann stability) — unchanged cross-reference; WIN-038 pole-only floor argument independently supports the Self-Contradicted verdict regardless of any storm-time framing

WIN-061.c4 holes remaining (not addressed by this EXP): H1 (TLDR-vs-detail contradiction — decider-applied already; current TLDRs match detail), H2 (detail_verdict_text contradiction — decider-applied already; current detail_verdict_text is long and consistent), H3 (timestamp-forward registration sentences — ISS-1418, still open), H4 (Unscored-vs-observed contradiction — decider-applied already; current paragraph 2 last sentence flags the contradiction), H6 ('most often cited' overreach — decider-applied already; current text reads 'often cited in the storm-time SR literature'), H7 (coin-flip overreach — decider-applied already; current text uses three-dimension framing), H8 (H_eff averaging method disclosure — ISS-1454, still open)
unaddressed_holes_note: EXP-232 scope is strictly ISS-1451 (c4 H5, moderate — SPE/storm category conflation in paragraphs 1 and 2). The c4 review flagged three additional issues currently assigned-analyst or open: (a) ISS-1454 (c4 H8 / c3 H7 carry-forward, moderate — H_eff averaging method still undisclosed in preserved baseline text; this is a content-gap issue, not a rewrite issue, and deserves its own small footnote EXP); (b) ISS-1418 (c3 H5 / c4 H3, major — PRED-SR-SUPPRESS timestamp-forward registration argument still absent from WIN-061 text; this is the sharpest remaining attack and deserves its own EXP, but it is not assigned to this run); (c) c4 H7 rhetorical 'coin-flip' overreach — already decider-applied per observation of current text. Also unused: the cross-WIN tensions with WIN-038 (stability window) and WIN-034 (copper firmament conductivity) that the c4 curmudgeon review flagged as 'free wins' if added to paragraph 2. Recommend decider queue follow-up EXPs for ISS-1454 (H_eff disclosure), ISS-1418 (timestamp-forward sentences), and the WIN-038/WIN-034 cross-WIN additions in a subsequent curmudgeon cycle if the verdict remains active.
notes_for_decider: Two atomic substring-replacement patches in WIN-061 detail_evidence. Both before-strings were verified unique in the current field value at EXP creation time (see 'expected_occurrences: 1' and 'verified: true' in each patch_anchor). IMPORTANT: PATCH A uses the '≲' character (U+2272, 'less-than or similar to') in 'Dst ≲ −70 nT'; this is a standard scientific notation character (used by Pazos et al. 2019 itself) and should be preserved as UTF-8 — do NOT auto-convert to '<' or '<=' or ASCII approximations. PATCH A also uses em-dashes (—, U+2014) in the mechanistic parenthetical, consistent with the rest of paragraph 1. PATCH B uses em-dash in '— and the genuine-CME-storm evidence' for paragraph-level style consistency. Character delta: PATCH A +455, PATCH B +159, total +614. Projected new detail_evidence length: 5138 (current: 4524). After integration: (a) run tests; (b) verify three DOI links resolve (10.1029/1999JA900056 still appears twice, 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 now appears twice, 10.1007/978-3-662-03137-4_11 unchanged); (c) the 'Section 2.1' cross-reference at the end of paragraph 1 (href='#part2' onclick='showTab(selftest)') is NOT touched by either patch and should remain unchanged; (d) consider queueing follow-up EXPs for ISS-1454 (H_eff averaging disclosure) and ISS-1418 (timestamp-forward registration) — both remain open and both are more verdict-durable than this polish-level SPE/storm fix. TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are intentionally unchanged (both already direction-mixed and consistent with the rewritten paragraphs). Character budget sanity: 5138 chars is approaching the long end for a WIN detail_evidence field but still within the observed range (WIN-010 detail_evidence is longer after recent expansions). If the decider prefers a tighter version, the SPE/storm mechanistic parenthetical in PATCH A is the optional-trim target — dropping it saves ~220 chars but weakens pre-emption against the mechanistic-oversimplification defender attack.
deeper_analysis:
ISS-1451 is a second-order consequence of EXP-227. EXP-227 correctly added the claim that S&F 1999 measured cavity-Q enhancement during solar proton events — this is the paper's actual finding. But by making 'solar proton events' explicit in the text, EXP-227 introduced a category distinction that the subsequent sentence structure did not engage: paragraph 1's top line is about G3+ geomagnetic storms, which are CME-driven electron-precipitation events, not SPEs (proton-precipitation events). The two storm classes have different energetics (SPE energies range ~10 MeV and up; CME-storm electrons are typically much softer), different altitude coupling (proton deeper, electron shallower into D-region), and different local-time and latitudinal signatures. A careful dome defender reading the post-EXP-227 paragraph 1 can argue that our rebuttal of storm-time suppression leads with an SPE study, which is a category error regardless of the direction of the finding.

The fix has three components: (1) Promote Pazos et al. 2019 — a genuine CME-driven G-class storm study — to the primary citation position. The Pazos study examined Dst ≲ −70 nT events at Coeneo over five 14-day storm periods and found statistically significant first-mode amplitude enhancement. This is the storm-time-enhancement evidence our argument actually needs. (2) Present S&F 1999 as a supporting citation for 'a distinct but related disturbance class', with the SPE label kept explicit. (3) Add an explicit mechanistic distinction (proton vs electron precipitation, mesospheric vs D-region coupling) in a parenthetical, pre-empting the defender's attack rather than relying on the reader to infer it.

Paragraph 2's fix is simpler: the current wording 'the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 paper often cited in the storm-time SR literature actually reports cavity-Q enhancement ... during SPEs' combines two categories ('storm-time SR literature' and 'during SPEs') in a way that invites conflation. The rewrite clarifies that the citation is for 'cavity-Q observations during high-energy particle precipitation' — a specific observational finding rather than a storm-time categorization — and adds a cross-reference to Pazos as the genuine CME-storm evidence. The underlying claim (S&F reports enhancement, not suppression) is preserved verbatim.

Alternative approaches considered and rejected: (1) Drop S&F entirely from paragraph 1 — rejected because S&F's enhancement finding is genuine and the SPE/storm mechanistic relationship is real, so dropping it sacrifices a legitimate supporting citation for a category-error fix that doesn't require it. (2) Swap S&F for a different storm-time enhancement paper (e.g., Sátori et al. 2005 on SR frequency during storm-sudden-commencement events) — rejected because S&F is already in the text and has been defended through two curmudgeon cycles; a wholesale citation swap introduces new verification burden and risks new errors. (3) Rewrite both paragraphs entirely — rejected as out-of-scope sprawl. The targeted two-patch approach matches the curmudgeon c4 recommendation literally and preserves verified text where possible.

On the other side of the argument (user preference, applied): A defender focused on debating points could still attack the rewritten paragraph 1 on three fronts. (A) 'Most direct storm-time evidence is a single-station single-paper citation from Coeneo Mexico; that's fragile.' — Response: The text says 'most direct', not 'definitive', and paragraph 1 invokes Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 as the mechanism-menu authority. Single-station-Coeneo is a real limitation, but the literature-balance point (enhancement vs suppression under different conditions) does not depend on Pazos being definitive. (B) 'The mechanistic parenthetical is oversimplified — real storm ionospheric response is mixed electron/proton populations, not a clean SPE/CME split.' — Response: Rewrite uses 'deepens to' and 'couples more strongly', which preserves heuristic framing. Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 mechanism-menu citation is the mechanistic-inventory authority. The SPE/CME distinction is a qualitative category distinction, sufficient for the paragraph's logical needs. (C) 'Adding 600+ characters to a 4500-char field is scope creep for a moderate-severity polish issue.' — Response: The length cost is real but the pre-emption is load-bearing — a defender who successfully lands the SPE/storm category-error attack can claim the paragraph's logic collapses, and the verdict holds by the ~22 Hz baseline argument alone. Pre-empting the attack in the paragraph where the confusion originates is simpler than carrying the defender objection into every subsequent review cycle.

Overall the verdict (Self-Contradicted) is unaffected. The ~22 Hz baseline overshoot, WIN-038 pole-only floor cross-reference, and V51.1 direction-and-magnitude-assertion framing all survive this patch unchanged. ISS-1451 was correctly classified as moderate severity (credibility polish, not verdict threatening), and this EXP closes it without introducing other holes.
tldr_change: None. tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict of WIN-061 currently frame the argument around the mixed-direction literature and the ~22 Hz baseline overshoot — which are reinforced rather than altered by the SPE/storm deconflation. tldr_evidence already reads 'Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 and Pazos et al. 2019 both report amplitude enhancement during storm conditions; other papers report suppression under different conditions' — a simplification that is layman-correct even though it papers over the SPE/storm distinction. Per analyst.md TLDR review standard ('Fix factual errors. Do not split hairs on nuance — the expanded detail handles that'), this is below the revision threshold. If the decider disagrees, a minimal tldr_evidence tweak is available: replace 'both report amplitude enhancement during storm conditions' with 'both report enhancement during the respective disturbance classes they studied' — but this adds jargon cost without adding layman value. Not recommended.
